passage_text:
Dr. Elara Vance, born in 1905 in Maine, was a pioneering marine biologist who revolutionized the study of deep-sea ecosystems. From a young age, Vance spent her days exploring tide pools, which ignited her lifelong passion for the ocean. Despite her parents' wishes for her to pursue a more traditional career, she earned a degree in marine biology from a prestigious university in 1927.

In 1932, Vance made her most significant contribution to science by developing the 'Aquasphere,' a heavily armored diving bell that allowed researchers to observe marine life at unprecedented depths. Using this groundbreaking invention, she discovered over fifty new species of deep-sea creatures. Her detailed observations were published in her 1935 book, Mysteries of the Deep, which became a massive success and brought her international fame.

However, Vance's journey was not without obstacles. During the 1940s, she struggled to secure financial backing for her expeditions due to the economic downturn and widespread skepticism from her male colleagues. Many scientists dismissed her findings, claiming that life could not survive in the extreme pressure of the deep ocean.

Undeterred, Vance established the Vance Marine Institute in 1950, dedicating the rest of her life to ocean conservation and funding young researchers. She passed away in 1980, leaving behind a legacy that continues to inspire modern marine biologists.

questions.quiz: What did Elara Vance's parents want her to do?
questions.commentary: 지문 첫 문단에 부모님이 전통적인 직업을 갖기를 원했다고 언급됨.

questions.quiz: What was the main purpose of the Aquasphere?
questions.answer3: To observe marine life at great depths
questions.commentary: 두 번째 단락에 심해 생물을 관찰하기 위한 도구라고 명시됨.

questions.quiz: What is true about Vance's book published in 1935?
questions.commentary: 두 번째 단락에 발견한 50종 이상의 생물 관찰 기록이 담겨 있음.

questions.quiz: Why did she have difficulty getting funding in the 1940s?
questions.commentary: 세 번째 단락에 경기 침체와 동료들의 회의론이 원인이라고 언급됨.

questions.quiz: What did Vance do in 1950?
questions.commentary: 마지막 단락에 1950년 해양 연구소를 설립했다고 나옴.

questions.quiz: The word 'groundbreaking' in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to:
questions.commentary: '획기적인'이라는 뜻으로 'innovative'와 동의어임.

questions.quiz: The word 'established' in paragraph 4 is closest in meaning to:
questions.commentary: '설립하다'라는 뜻으로 'founded'와 동의어임.

passage_text:
Julian Sterling, born in 1888 in a small village in France, was a revolutionary painter who paved the way for modern Impressionism. Growing up in a modest household, Sterling was initially discouraged by his father from pursuing art, with the family insisting he study medicine instead. Nevertheless, Sterling spent his nights sketching the local landscape, driven by an undeniable creative urge that would eventually define his career.

In 1912, Sterling achieved his major breakthrough when he began experimenting with light and shadow in his outdoor paintings. He developed a technique that focused on capturing the fleeting quality of light, which he termed 'Luminous Impression.' His first major exhibition in 1915 attracted thousands of visitors and brought him immediate fame, though traditional critics remained skeptical of his unconventional methods.

However, Sterling faced immense personal and professional hardship during the First World War. His studio was destroyed by an air raid in 1917, and he lost a vast collection of his early sketches. During this difficult time, his mental health suffered, and many doubted he would ever produce a masterpiece again.

Sterling eventually returned to his art with renewed vigor, producing his most iconic works in the 1920s. He later dedicated his remaining years to teaching young artists at the Paris Academy of Fine Arts before passing away in 1955. His legacy remains a cornerstone of 20th-century art.

questions.quiz: What career path did Julian Sterling's father originally want him to follow?
questions.commentary: 첫 번째 단락에서 가족들이 의학(medicine)을 공부하기를 고집했다고 언급되었습니다.

questions.quiz: What is the 'Luminous Impression' technique known for?
questions.answer1: Capturing the fleeting quality of light
questions.commentary: 두 번째 단락에서 빛의 일시적인 특성을 포착(capturing the fleeting quality of light)하는 기법이라고 설명됩니다.

questions.quiz: How did traditional critics react to Sterling's early exhibition?
questions.commentary: 두 번째 단락에서 전통적인 비평가들이 그의 파격적인 방법에 회의적(skeptical)이었다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: What major obstacle occurred in 1917?
questions.answer1: His studio was destroyed by an air raid.
questions.answer2: He was drafted into the army.
questions.commentary: 세 번째 단락에서 1917년 공습으로 스튜디오가 파괴되었다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: What did Sterling do in his later years?
questions.answer1: He taught at the Paris Academy of Fine Arts.
questions.commentary: 마지막 단락에서 파리 예술 아카데미에서 젊은 예술가들을 가르쳤다고 나옵니다.

questions.commentary: '겸손한, 보통의, 소박한'이라는 뜻으로 'humble'이 가장 적절합니다.

questions.commentary: '활력, 정력'이라는 뜻으로 'energy'가 가장 적절합니다.

passage_text:
Marcus Montague, born in 1895 in Italy, emerged as one of the most influential political reformers of his time. Born into a family of laborers, Montague witnessed the systemic inequality in his community, which inspired him to enter the world of law. He aimed to challenge the corrupt political machinery that burdened the working class with unfair taxes.

Montague's greatest political victory came in 1928 with the passage of the Labor Protection Act. This legislation set unprecedented standards for workplace safety and minimum wages, directly benefiting millions of workers across the nation. His ability to negotiate with rival parties was key to passing such a controversial bill, earning him a reputation as a master diplomat.

However, Montague’s rise to power sparked fierce opposition from the political elite. In 1934, he faced a massive smear campaign orchestrated by his opponents, who accused him of corruption to undermine his popularity. The scandal forced him to resign from his position, leaving his reform movement in a state of chaos.

Despite the setback, Montague continued to write and speak out against inequality until his death in 1970. His life is still studied by historians as a symbol of integrity and commitment to social justice.

questions.quiz: What motivated Montague to study law?
questions.answer3: A desire to become a politician
questions.commentary: 첫 번째 단락에서 노동자 계층의 구조적 불평등을 목격하고 법조계로 진출하게 되었다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: What was the result of the Labor Protection Act?
questions.answer1: Improved workplace safety and higher wages
questions.commentary: 두 번째 단락에 직장 안전과 최저임금의 기준을 세워 노동자들에게 혜택을 주었다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: How did Montague manage to pass the controversial bill?
questions.commentary: 두 번째 단락에서 경쟁 정당과의 협상 능력이 법안 통과의 핵심이었다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: Why did Montague resign in 1934?
questions.answer1: Because of a corruption scandal smear campaign
questions.answer3: He was offered a new position
questions.commentary: 세 번째 단락에서 적들에 의해 기획된 비리 스캔들 때문에 사임했다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: What is the modern view of Marcus Montague?
questions.answer1: A symbol of integrity and social justice
questions.commentary: 마지막 단락에서 그를 사회 정의에 대한 헌신과 청렴함의 상징으로 여긴다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: The word 'undermine' in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to:
questions.commentary: '약화시키다'는 뜻으로 'weaken'이 동의어입니다.

passage_text:
Sylvia Hawthorne, an ecologist born in 1900 in Canada, spent her life defending the vast forests of the North. As a child, Hawthorne lived in a cabin where she developed a deep bond with the wilderness. While her family wanted her to focus on nursing, she chose to study forest ecology, believing that the future of the planet depended on the health of its natural resources.

Her defining moment arrived in 1938, when she spearheaded the campaign to save the Great Northern Forest from industrial logging. She organized thousands of citizens to lobby the government, eventually succeeding in designating the area as a protected national reserve. Her success made her a hero to nature lovers everywhere.

However, Hawthorne’s life was met with significant backlash from powerful industrial corporations. In 1945, these companies launched a massive media campaign to discredit her, claiming her conservation efforts would ruin the local economy. For several years, she was barred from speaking at major universities.

Despite these trials, Hawthorne continued her work quietly. She established a forest research center in 1952, which focuses on sustainable land management. She remained a dedicated activist until she passed away in 1975, and her efforts are still a benchmark for modern conservationists.

questions.quiz: What was Hawthorne's family's original wish for her?
questions.commentary: 첫 번째 단락에서 가족들이 그녀가 간호학에 집중하기를 바랐다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: What was the result of Hawthorne's 1938 campaign?
questions.answer1: The forest became a protected national reserve.
questions.answer2: She was fired from her university.
questions.answer3: The forest was cleared for logging.
questions.answer4: She was given a government job.
questions.commentary: 두 번째 단락에서 그 지역이 보호된 국립 공원으로 지정되었다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: How did corporations react to Hawthorne’s conservation efforts?
questions.commentary: 세 번째 단락에서 기업들이 그녀를 깎아내리기 위한 미디어 캠페인을 펼쳤다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: What happened to Hawthorne during the late 1940s?
questions.answer1: She was not allowed to speak at universities.
questions.commentary: 세 번째 단락에 몇 년 동안 주요 대학에서 연설하는 것이 금지되었다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: What did the research center established in 1952 focus on?
questions.commentary: 마지막 단락에서 해당 연구소가 지속 가능한 토지 관리에 중점을 둔다고 나옵니다.

questions.commentary: '기준, 표준'이라는 뜻으로 'standard'가 동의어입니다.

passage_text:
Victor Caldwell, a visionary astronomer born in 1875 in Germany, changed how humanity perceives the night sky. From a young age, he spent nights observing constellations, yet his father, a wealthy merchant, insisted that he prepare for a career in law. Victor managed to study astronomy in secret, often staying awake until dawn to map the stars.

Caldwell's breakthrough came in 1905, when he developed a new method for calculating comet trajectories. By using advanced mathematics, he could predict the exact return times of rare comets. His findings were initially met with indifference by the scientific community, but they were soon verified by world-renowned experts, catapulting him to international fame.

Tragedy struck in 1912, when a lab fire destroyed many of his primary research journals and charts. This event set his work back significantly, as it took years for him to reconstruct the lost data from memory. During this time, he struggled with severe depression and nearly abandoned his research altogether.

He emerged stronger in 1920, opening a public observatory to encourage common people to engage with science. He continued his contributions to astronomy until his peaceful passing in 1947. Today, his formulas remain essential for space missions.

questions.quiz: What did Victor Caldwell's father want him to become?
questions.commentary: 첫 번째 단락에서 부모님이 그에게 법조계로 진출할 준비를 하라고 고집했다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: What did Caldwell discover in 1905?
questions.answer1: A method to calculate comet trajectories
questions.answer2: A new planet in the solar system
questions.answer3: A way to see through clouds
questions.answer4: A faster method of building telescopes
questions.commentary: 두 번째 단락에 혜성 궤도를 계산하는 새로운 방법을 개발했다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: How did the scientific community first react to Caldwell’s findings?
questions.commentary: 두 번째 단락에 초기에는 무관심(indifference)으로 반응했다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: What was the consequence of the 1912 lab fire?
questions.answer3: He was injured in the fire.
questions.commentary: 세 번째 단락에 화재로 주요 연구 저널과 차트가 파괴되었다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: What did Caldwell establish in 1920?
questions.commentary: 마지막 단락에서 일반인들이 과학을 접할 수 있도록 공공 천문대를 열었다고 나옵니다.

passage_text:
Clara Bennett, an English writer born in 1890, was famous for novels that challenged the social norms of the early 20th century. Born into a wealthy family, Bennett was expected to lead a quiet life of leisure. Instead, she began writing anonymous stories, using a masculine pen name to ensure her work was judged on its own merit.

Her debut novel, released in 1920, shocked the public with its raw portrayal of working-class struggles. Despite the controversy, the book was a best-seller, exposing the hardships of urban laborers to the wealthy middle class. Bennett's sharp critique of industrialization became a model for later social activists.

Bennett faced a significant crisis in the late 1920s when her identity was exposed against her will. Many family members cut ties with her, and she was blacklisted by several major publishers. This period of isolation led her to produce some of her most introspective and poetic works, which gained a cult following in later years.

She continued to be a voice for the voiceless until the end of her life in 1960. Today, she is honored as one of the most courageous figures in literature.

questions.quiz: Why did Bennett use a masculine pen name?
questions.answer1: So her work would be judged on its own merit
questions.answer4: Because she did not like her own name
questions.commentary: 첫 번째 단락에서 남성 필명을 사용하여 작품 자체의 가치로 평가받고 싶어 했다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: What was the subject of Bennett's 1920 debut novel?
questions.commentary: 두 번째 단락에 노동자 계층의 투쟁을 생생하게 묘사했다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: What happened to Bennett in the late 1920s?
questions.answer1: Her real identity was revealed publicly.
questions.answer4: She was arrested for her writing.
questions.commentary: 세 번째 단락에 그녀의 신원이 원치 않게 밝혀졌다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: How did Bennett's isolation in the late 1920s influence her work?
questions.answer1: It led to more introspective and poetic writings.
questions.answer2: She lost the ability to write stories.
questions.answer3: It forced her to quit writing for money.
questions.answer4: She started writing for children only.
questions.commentary: 세 번째 단락에 그 시기의 고립이 더 내성적이고 시적인 작품을 쓰게 했다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: What is Clara Bennett remembered as today?
questions.commentary: 마지막 단락에서 문학계에서 가장 용기 있는 인물로 존경받는다고 나옵니다.

questions.quiz: The word 'anonymous' in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to:
questions.commentary: '익명의, 이름이 없는'이라는 뜻이므로 'nameless'가 가장 적절합니다.
